eli3: SVD decomposes any matrix into three simpler matrices: A = UΣVᵀ. Think of it as breaking any transformation into rotate → stretch → rotate. U and V are rotation matrices; Σ is a diagonal stretching matrix.

Every matrix, even non-square ones, has an SVD. The diagonal entries of Σ (singular values) tell you how much stretching happens along each principal direction. They're always non-negative and sorted from largest to smallest.

The columns of U are the output directions; columns of V are the input directions. Together they give you the best low-rank approximation of the matrix — just keep the top k singular values and drop the rest.

whenWhy.title: Dimensionality reduction and compression
whenWhy.body: Keeping the top k singular values gives the best rank-k approximation of A (Eckart-Young-Mirsky theorem). Used in image compression, recommender systems (Netflix prize), and latent semantic analysis.

whenWhy.title: Solving ill-conditioned linear systems
whenWhy.body: For Ax = b with noisy data, SVD reveals when the system is nearly singular (small singular values). Truncating small singular values (regularization) stabilizes the solution. This is Tikhonov regularization in disguise.

whenWhy.title: Principal Component Analysis
whenWhy.body: For centered data matrix X, the right singular vectors V are the principal components. Σ²/(n-1) are the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix. SVD is numerically more stable than explicit eigendecomposition of XᵀX.

whenWhy.title: Matrix completion and collaborative filtering
whenWhy.body: In recommender systems, we fill missing entries by assuming a low-rank structure. SVD approximations (with regularization) predict user ratings. Variants like Funk SVD and biased SVD won the Netflix prize.

deepDiveIntro: Any matrix A ∈ ℝᵐˣⁿ can be factored as A = UΣVᵀ where U ∈ ℝᵐˣᵐ and V ∈ ℝⁿˣⁿ are orthogonal matrices (UᵀU = I, VᵀV = I), and Σ ∈ ℝᵐˣⁿ is diagonal with non-negative entries σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ … ≥ σᵣ > 0 where r = rank(A).

The columns of U (left singular vectors) form an orthonormal basis for the column space and left null space. The columns of V (right singular vectors) form an orthonormal basis for the row space and null space. The singular values are the square roots of eigenvalues of AᵀA and AAᵀ.

deepDive.heading: Singular Value Decomposition
deepDive.body: A = UΣVᵀ. U contains eigenvectors of AAᵀ; V contains eigenvectors of AᵀA; Σ contains square roots of eigenvalues of both (same nonzero eigenvalues). The factorization exists for any matrix, even rectangular or rank-deficient.
deepDive.formula: A = UΣVᵀ, UᵀU = I, VᵀV = I, Σ = diag(σ₁,…,σᵣ)

deepDive.heading: Low-Rank Approximation
deepDive.body: The best rank-k approximation (in Frobenius or spectral norm) is Aₖ = UₖΣₖVₖᵀ keeping top k singular values. Error = σₖ₊₁. This is the Eckart-Young-Mirsky theorem. Compression ratio: store k(m+n+1) vs mn numbers.

deepDive.heading: Pseudo-inverse
deepDive.body: The Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse A⁺ = VΣ⁺Uᵀ where Σ⁺ inverts nonzero singular values. A⁺ gives the minimum-norm least-squares solution to Ax = b: x = A⁺b. Essential for underdetermined or overdetermined systems.
deepDive.formula: A⁺ = VΣ⁺Uᵀ, x = A⁺b minimizes ||Ax - b||² and ||x||²

deepDive.heading: Condition Number
deepDive.body: κ(A) = σₘₐₓ/σₘᵢₙ measures sensitivity of linear systems. Large κ means small errors in b cause large errors in x. In double precision, expect to lose log₁₀(κ) digits of accuracy. κ > 10¹⁶ is essentially singular.

deepDive.heading: Computing SVD
deepDive.body: Algorithms: Golub-Kahan bidiagonalization followed by QR iteration or divide-and-conquer. Complexity is O(mn²) for m ≥ n. For large matrices, use randomized SVD (Halko et al.) which samples column space with random projections.

takeaways: SVD = UΣVᵀ: rotate → stretch → rotate. Exists for any matrix.
Singular values σᵢ are square roots of eigenvalues of AᵀA.
Best rank-k approximation: keep top k singular values (Eckart-Young-Mirsky).
Pseudo-inverse A⁺ = VΣ⁺Uᵀ solves least squares problems.
Condition number κ = σₘₐₓ/σₘᵢₙ measures numerical stability.

edgeCases.q: What happens when singular values are zero?
edgeCases.a: Zero singular values indicate rank deficiency. The matrix collapses some dimensions to zero. The pseudo-inverse inverts only nonzero singular values (setting 1/0 = 0 by convention), giving the minimum-norm solution in the row space.

edgeCases.q: Why is SVD preferred over eigendecomposition for PCA?
edgeCases.a: Computing XᵀX explicitly squares the condition number, amplifying numerical errors. SVD of X directly is stable. Also, SVD works when n < p (more features than samples), where XᵀX is singular.

edgeCases.q: When should I use truncated SVD vs full SVD?
edgeCases.a: Truncated SVD (computing only top k components) is O(mnk) vs O(mn²) for full SVD. Use truncated when you only need a low-rank approximation or when storage is limited. Randomized methods work well for this.

sources.label: Strang — Introduction to Linear Algebra (6th ed.), Ch. 7
Golub & Van Loan — Matrix Computations (4th ed.)
